problem_summary: 2019 MCM Problem B ("Send in the Drones" / DroneGo): design a transportable disaster-response system for post-Hurricane Maria Puerto Rico. Given a fixed 40-ft ISO cargo container, a fleet of 8 candidate rotor-drone types (A–H, each with distinct dimensions, payload, speed, flight time), 3 medical-package types (MED1/2/3), and matching cargo bays, the team must decide: (1) how many containers, (2) which drones and how many to pack in each, (3) the 3D packing configuration, (4) where to physically locate containers in Puerto Rico, and (5) a flight schedule that delivers medicine to 5 hospitals while maximizing road/area reconnaissance. It is a discrete combinatorial selection + bin-packing + facility-location + scheduling problem.
models_used: AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) for weighting 3 decision criteria — built a 3x3 pairwise comparison matrix, derived weights (0.2051 delivery time, 0.7167 medical supply amount, 0.0783 reconnaissance) via principal eigenvector, validated with consistency check (lambda_max=3.0183, CR=0.0158 < 0.1)

Normalized Comprehensive Evaluation (CE) scoring function: CE = w_f*f(D) + w_g*g(n) + w_h*h(x_n), each sub-score mapped to [0,1]; used a saturating marking function f(D)=D/(D+80) for medical supply so 120 supply-units scores 0.6 and infinity asymptotes to 1

Greedy algorithm for drone-mix selection: add drones one at a time, each addition chosen to maximize CE; exploited the observed unimodal (increase-then-decrease) shape of CE vs. drone count to stop at the peak — yielded 121 type-B, 54 type-D, 3 type-H drones

3D Bin-Packing (3DBP) solved via an external commercial API (3dbinpacking.com) rather than a hand-rolled NP-hard solver — used to get realistic per-container space-utilization (achieved 77.6%/89.34%/81.64%, avg 82.86%)

Bisection / binary search wrapped around the 3DBP API to find the maximum feasible number of supply-days that still packs into the 3 containers (binary-searched day count in [330,370], plus nested binary searches on MED quantities per container)

Image-based / pixel-discretization facility-location model: rendered roads, populated places, and drone-range circles to raster images in MATLAB, discretized into pixels, then scored every candidate pixel inside the feasible region by a weighted sum (0.6*road-pixel density + 0.4*populated-place density) to pick exact container lat/lon

Geometric range analysis with circle-intersection reasoning to set the rough container regions (delivery-and-return radius x_iR derived from speed*time/(1/0.8 + 1), container-to-container transfer radius x_BS)
Closed-form delivery-time model T = D*V_m / sum(z_i / (d_i/v_i + T_0)) simplified to T proportional to 1/n_d under justified constant-payload/constant-flight-time assumptions
Cyclic/periodic flight scheduling — hand-constructed a steady-state repeating 2-hour flight cycle per container to deliver all medicine in 5 days
key_techniques: Multi-criteria decision framing: collapsed a messy 'what is a good plan' question into 3 measurable axes (delivery time, supply amount, reconnaissance) and made AHP weight them explicitly with a consistency check — turns subjective judgment into defensible numbers

Exploiting problem structure to justify a cheap algorithm: argued CE is unimodal in drone count so greedy hill-climbing finds the global optimum, avoiding exhaustive search over an enormous discrete space
Outsourcing the NP-hard core: rather than implement a 3D bin packer, called a battle-tested commercial API and spent their effort on the decision layer wrapping it (bisection over feasibility)

Two-stage location solve: coarse geometric/circle analysis to bound feasible regions, then fine pixel-discretization optimization within those regions — reduces a continuous 2D search to a tractable scored grid

Encoding geography as images and processing with scikit-image: roads/cities/ranges become PNG layers, min-pooled to downsample, then density-scored per pixel — a creative discrete proxy for a GIS optimization
Saturating utility functions (D/(D+80)) to encode diminishing returns and anchor a meaningful operating point (120 days = 4 months baseline)

Sensitivity analysis on the two most assumption-laden levers: the 80% distance-reduction factor (perturbed +/-0.025) and the AHP weights (perturbed each axis) — showed location shifts are negligible and drone mix stays B/D-only
Reproducible deliverables: included actual runnable C (greedy), Python (3DBP+bisection), MATLAB and scikit-image code in appendices, plus a CEO letter/memo
why_it_won: "It won because it cleanly decomposed a sprawling open-ended logistics problem into a pipeline of named, individually-defensible sub-models (AHP weights -> normalized CE -> greedy drone selection -> 3DBP packing -> geometric+pixel location -> cyclic schedule) and then actually executed every stage end-to-end with real numbers and real code, instead of hand-waving any link. Judges reward teams that (a) make the 'what counts as good' criterion explicit and validated — here AHP with a passing consistency ratio — and (b) close the loop from abstract optimization all the way to concrete, checkable outputs: exact lat/lon coordinates, per-container packing tables with space-utilization percentages, and an hour-by-hour 5-day flight timetable. The team was also pragmatically honest about NP-hardness, offloading the bin-packing to a proven API and wrapping it in bisection rather than pretending to solve it exactly. Sensitivity analysis on the two shakiest assumptions (the 80% range factor and the AHP weights) demonstrated robustness. The result is a model that is simultaneously ambitious in scope and fully delivered — the hallmark of an Outstanding paper."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet is strong: states the full answer (3 containers, drone counts, packing, schedule) and names every method (AHP, normalization, greedy, image analysis, bisection, 3DBP) in one page. Section 4 ('Specific formulation') with the mermaid-style flow diagram orients the reader to the whole solution pipeline before diving in — excellent roadmapping. Section 5 (AHP) is rigorous: it not only gives weights but justifies WHY each criterion matters and reports the consistency ratio. Section 5.4's normalization is a highlight — explicit marking functions with reasoned anchor points (120 units = 0.6 mark). Section 6 transparently transitions from 'ideal' packing (411 days) to 'reality' (acknowledging ~20% packing waste, then noting 411 >> 120 so it doesn't matter). The location sections (6.2, 7) pair an intuitive annotated map (color-coded delivery-range circles) with a quantitative pixel-density optimization. The CEO letter/memo translates everything into stakeholder language. Appendices A.1–A.4 ship the actual greedy C code, Python 3DBP+bisection driver, and MATLAB/scikit-image location code — reproducibility judges love."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The single most consequential assumption — that loaded drones reach exactly 80% of their range — is asserted, not derived; the whole location and scheduling layer rides on it. They tested +/-0.025 but never grounded the 80% itself in physics or the problem data.

Uniform distance-reduction rate across all drone types is physically wrong (the team admits this in Weaknesses): reduction depends on payload/weight ratio, which differs hugely between type B and type D.

AHP weights are inherently subjective — the 4:1:8-style pairwise judgments driving 0.7167 to medical supply are asserted by the team; a different (also-reasonable) matrix would change the drone mix, as their own sensitivity bar chart shows D-count swinging ~33%.

The greedy global-optimality claim rests on CE being unimodal, which is argued informally (several 'we can reasonably assume' simplifications: T proportional to 1/n_d, constant average payload past 10 drones) rather than proven — greedy could in principle miss the true optimum.
The flat-island / ignore-altitude assumption is flagged but unquantified; Puerto Rico's central mountains materially affect both flight distance and line-of-sight reconnaissance.
Reconnaissance ability is modeled crudely as proportional to total max flight distance, not actual area/road coverage — a weak proxy that conveniently gets the smallest weight, somewhat circular.
The appendix code is visibly rough/incomplete (OCR'd fragments, a corrupted function name, truncated lines), and the bin volumes/API item lists hardcode magic numbers — hard to audit or rerun as-is.

411-day ideal vs 359-day delivered gap and the 'roughly 20% less' packing-waste estimate are eyeballed, not measured; real container loading constraints (stacking, fragility, access) are abstracted away.

Numbers are slightly inconsistent across the paper (e.g., container counts reported as 121 B / 42 D in conclusion vs 121 B / 54 D from greedy vs 99 B total in the packing table), suggesting the ideal-to-reality reconciliation wasn't fully tightened.
reusable_patterns: AHP-to-CE pipeline: when a problem asks 'find the best X' without defining 'best', pick 2–4 measurable criteria, weight them with an AHP pairwise matrix, REPORT the consistency ratio (CR<0.1) to prove the weights are coherent, then normalize each criterion to [0,1] and combine into one comprehensive score to optimize. Highly transferable to any selection/design MCM problem.

Greedy + unimodality argument: if your objective plausibly rises then falls as you add a discrete resource, add items one at a time maximizing the score and stop at the peak — but explicitly argue why the objective is unimodal so judges accept the global-optimum claim.

Outsource the NP-hard kernel, own the decision layer: for bin-packing/TSP/scheduling cores, call a proven library/API and spend your modeling effort on the feasibility wrapper (here: bisection search over the target metric). Judges reward correct results over reinvented solvers.

Bisection-over-a-feasibility-oracle: to maximize a scalar (days of supply) subject to a black-box feasibility test (does it pack?), binary-search the scalar and call the oracle at each midpoint. Generalizes to any 'largest feasible' question.

Two-stage coarse-then-fine location: bound the feasible region with cheap geometric/range reasoning (intersecting coverage circles), THEN run a fine optimization (pixel grid scored by weighted feature density) only inside that region.

Geography-as-image: rasterize map features into layered PNGs, discretize to pixels, and score candidate sites by weighted feature density — a lightweight GIS substitute when you lack real geo tooling.
Saturating utility functions (x/(x+c)) to encode diminishing returns with a calibrated anchor point, instead of linear scoring that over-rewards extremes.

Sensitivity-test your shakiest assumptions, not random ones: identify the 2–3 parameters/judgments your answer most depends on (here the range factor and AHP weights), perturb them, and show the answer is stable.

Deliverable discipline: lead with a complete-answer summary, include a solution-pipeline flow diagram early, end with a stakeholder letter/memo, and ship runnable code in appendices for reproducibility.
